game_name: Romance of the Three Kingdoms II

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: player chooses from 6 historical scenarios depicting different power configurations in ancient China
player selects which of 200+ unique characters to control, establishing initial power base
Intelligence, War Ability, and Charm stats (10-100 scale) determine success in dueling, diplomacy, and provincial development
managing 41 provinces as primary objective; land value increases through character placement
affects officer loyalty rates toward lords, introducing political complexity beyond military conquest
advisers predict success likelihood; Intelligence stat of 100 grants perfect accuracy
items found/acquired increase officer statistics, adding resource discovery layer
offensive espionage mechanic enabling player to disrupt opponent communications
players can insert custom lords into unoccupied territories, enabling dynasty building
winning condition requires controlling all 41 provinces; escalating challenge as game progresses

typical_tension_triggers: inability to increase provincial land value
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Long-form strategic pacing with multiple concurrent tension vectors; players juggle military conquest, diplomatic loyalty, resource development, and espionage simultaneously across 41-province map

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Custom character placement in unoccupied territories; optional treasures discoverable throughout provinces

difficulty_curve.early_game: Moderate; player establishes initial warlord control, learns character stat system and basic provincial mechanics
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Increasing complexity; reputation system, adviser mechanics, and multi-front territorial conflicts create parallel challenge layers
difficulty_curve.late_game: High; endgame requires simultaneous management of loyalty across many officers, espionage defense, and conquest of well-defended final territories
notable_spikes: Introduction of reputation system creates cascading loyalty management complexity
Messenger interception availability enables disruptive opponent actions
Final territorial push against consolidated rival powers

notable_level_patterns.description: Six distinct historical scenarios (189 AD through 220 AD) represent different starting territorial configurations and power distributions. Each scenario is effectively a separate campaign with unique challenge sequencing.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: AD 189 (Dong Zhuo seizes Luoyang) - earliest; AD 220 (Wei/Wu/Shu rise) - latest with three established powers

notable_level_patterns.description: Tension builds from juggling five concurrent systems: territorial control, officer recruitment/retention, stat development, reputation/loyalty, and espionage. No single dominant strategy mitigates all pressures.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Mid-game scenarios where player controls 10-25 provinces

notable_level_patterns.description: As game progresses, Computer Gaming World noted it 'simulates the chaos' of second-century China better than predecessor. Rivals exploit same adviser predictions, reputation mechanics, and espionage systems as player.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Late scenarios (215+ AD) with entrenched rival powers
